question_text: Evaluate the integral $\int x^2 e^x \, dx$ using integration by parts (apply twice).

steps.description: Set up the first integration by parts: choose u = x^2 and dv = e^x dx
steps.explanation: Which part gets simpler when you differentiate?
steps.work: u = x^2, \quad du = 2x \, dx, \quad dv = e^x dx, \quad v = e^x
steps.reinforcement: Good choice — x^2 simplifies when differentiated.
steps.tutor_speech: Set up integration by parts. Choose u and dv.

steps.description: Apply the formula uv - integral of v du for the first application
steps.explanation: Plug into uv - integral(v du). What do you get?
steps.reinforcement: First application of IBP done — now you have a simpler integral.
steps.tutor_speech: Apply the integration by parts formula.

steps.description: Set up the second integration by parts on the remaining integral: u = 2x, dv = e^x dx
steps.explanation: The remaining integral still has a polynomial times e^x. Apply IBP again.
steps.work: u = 2x, \quad du = 2 \, dx, \quad dv = e^x dx, \quad v = e^x
steps.reinforcement: Second IBP setup — one more application and you're done.
steps.tutor_speech: Set up integration by parts again on the remaining integral.

steps.description: Apply the formula again and combine with the first result
steps.explanation: Substitute the second IBP result back into the first expression.
steps.work: x^2 e^x - (2x e^x - \int 2 e^x \, dx) = x^2 e^x - 2x e^x + 2e^x
steps.reinforcement: Nice — substitution back into the first expression is the tricky part.
steps.tutor_speech: Apply the formula again and substitute back.

steps.description: Simplify and add the constant of integration
steps.explanation: Factor out e^x if you can. Don't forget +C.
steps.reinforcement: Perfect — factored form with the constant. That's a complete answer.
steps.tutor_speech: Simplify and add the constant of integration.
